title: Psoriatic Arthritis
oneLiner: Inflammatory arthritis in patients with psoriasis — often oligoarticular, with dactylitis and enthesitis; can erode joints and cause disability if missed.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 35-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of pain and swelling in her right index finger and left knee. She has a scaly, silvery rash on her elbows and scalp, which she’s had for years. On exam, the finger is diffusely swollen (“sausage digit”) and tender along the tendon sheath. There is tenderness over the insertion of the left Achilles tendon. She reports morning stiffness >30 minutes but feels better after moving. CRP is mildly elevated (15 mg/L), RF and anti-CCP are negative.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Scaly, silvery rash on elbows/scalp** → psoriasis (often precedes arthritis by years)
- **Swelling in one finger, entire digit** → dactylitis (“sausage digit”) — hallmark of PsA, distinct from RA
- **Tenderness at Achilles insertion** → enthesitis (another PsA hallmark, uncommon in RA or OA)
- **Morning stiffness >30 min** → inflammatory arthritis (versus mechanical pain of OA)
- **Asymmetric oligoarthritis** (right index + left knee) → typical PsA pattern (vs symmetric small joint RA)
- **RF/anti-CCP negative** → seronegative spondyloarthropathy; helps rule out RA
- **CRP mildly raised** → active inflammation, but can be normal in PsA

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Axial-only PsA** (~5%): presents as inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness, improves with exercise, no improvement with rest) with no peripheral joint involvement. Can be mistaken for ankylosing spondylitis — look for psoriasis (often hidden in scalp, gluteal cleft, or nails) and check HLA-B27 (positive in ~40% of PsA with axial disease).
- **PsA sine psoriasis** (~15%): arthritis typical of PsA (dactylitis, enthesitis, nail pitting) but no skin lesions at presentation. Family history of psoriasis or nail changes (pitting, onycholysis) are key clues.
- **Juvenile-onset PsA**: presents with oligoarthritis and dactylitis in a child, often before skin psoriasis appears. Can be misdiagnosed as JIA — nail pitting and family history essential.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Psoriatic arthritis is a chronic inflammatory arthritis that affects people with psoriasis (a common skin condition). It causes joint pain, stiffness, and swelling, and if untreated can lead to permanent joint damage and disability. It also affects the entheses (where tendons attach to bone) and can cause spondylitis.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Affects up to 30% of people with psoriasis (global prevalence ~0.3–1% of population). Many patients with psoriasis do not know they have arthritis — you must actively screen.
- **Stakes:** Untreated, it leads to erosive, deforming arthritis (arthritis mutilans), significant functional loss, reduced quality of life, and increased cardiovascular risk (systemic inflammation).
- **Common pitfalls:**
  - Mistaking dactylitis for gout or infection.
  - Assuming arthritis in a patient with psoriasis is osteoarthritis (age-related) or rheumatoid arthritis — get X-rays and serology correct.
  - Missing axial disease (inflammatory back pain) because patient doesn’t mention back — ask.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**  
Psoriatic arthritis sits within the spondyloarthropathy family — diseases driven by **interleukin-17 (IL-17)** and **IL-23** produced by Th17 cells. In a genetically susceptible person (HLA-Cw6, HLA-B27, other loci), environmental triggers (trauma, infection, stress) activate dendritic cells that present self-peptides (e.g., from cartilage) to T cells. This creates an IL-23/IL-17 axis: IL-23 drives Th17 cell expansion, which then secretes IL-17. IL-17 recruits neutrophils and stimulates fibroblasts and osteoclasts, causing **synovitis, enthesitis, and new bone formation**.

**Mechanism → sign:**
- **IL-17 driven inflammation** → synovitis → swollen, warm joint.
- **Enthesitis** (IL-17 stimulates inflammation at the osteotendinous junction) → pain at Achilles, plantar fascia, etc.
- **Dactylitis**: inflammation throughout a whole digit — synovitis, tenosynovitis, and enthesitis simultaneously — causing the "sausage" swelling.
- **New bone formation** (periostitis, syndesmophytes) — paradoxically, PsA causes both **erosion** (by osteoclast activation via RANKL) and **proliferation** (by IL-17-driven osteoblast activity). This is unique: in RA you see only erosion; in PsA you see erosion AND bony outgrowths, especially in the hands/feet (pencil-in-cup deformities) and spine (asymmetric paravertebral ossification).
- **Nail disease** (pitting, onycholysis, hyperkeratosis): the nail is a modified skin appendage; IL-17-driven inflammation in the nail matrix causes the same psoriatic pathology — hence nail psoriasis correlates strongly with enthesitis/arthritis.

**Connect to pattern:**
- Asymmetric oligoarthritis → because PsA tends to involve a limited number of "target" joints, often in a streaming pattern (e.g., one hand and the opposite knee). Reason unknown but thought to be related to triggered memory T cells homing to sites of previous microtrauma.
- Erythematous scaly plaques → skin inflammation also driven by IL-23/Th17 axis, but arthritis can occur without severe skin disease (cytokine trafficking differences).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | No clear trigger; genetic predisposition + random event | Patient with long-standing psoriasis develops joint pain for no apparent reason. |
| **Infectious** | Streptococcal pharyngitis, HIV, trauma? (post-traumatic PsA) | "Had a sore throat 2 weeks ago" — gut microbiome also implicated. |
| **Trauma** | "Deep Koebner" phenomenon — physical injury precipitates arthritis in a predisposed joint | "Pain started after I twisted my ankle" — but systemic signs later. |
| **Drugs** (rare) | TNF inhibitors used for other diseases (paradoxical PsA) | Anti-TNF therapy for Crohn's → new onset PsA. |
| **Endocrine** (rare) | Post-pregnancy, stress | "Arthritis started while under huge work stress." |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic, in a patient with existing psoriasis. About 75% have skin disease before arthritis. Screening every psoriatic patient for joint symptoms = key.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Use the CASPAR criteria for PsA classification (most specific):
- Patient must have inflammatory articular disease (joint, spine, or entheseal) **AND** ≥3 of:
  - Current psoriasis (2 points) OR personal history of psoriasis (1) OR family history (1)
  - Psoriatic nail dystrophy (pitting, onycholysis, hyperkeratosis) — 1 point
  - Negative RF — 1 point
  - Dactylitis (current or history) — 1 point
  - Radiographic evidence of juxta-articular new bone formation (on X-ray of hand/foot) — 1 point
- Score ≥3 points = classified as PsA.

**Test-by-test pattern:**
| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **RF, anti-CCP** | Autoantibodies | Rule out RA | Negative in >90% PsA (but can be false positive in older patients — use clinical judgment) |
| **CRP / ESR** | Inflammatory markers | Assess activity, monitor treatment | Raised in active disease; can be normal in ~40% — don't rely on normal CRP to exclude PsA |
| **HLA-B27** | Genetic marker | Suspect axial disease; prognostic | Positive in 40% with axial PsA (vs 90% in AS); has limited diagnostic value alone |
| **X-ray hands/feet** | Erosions, new bone formation | Detect joint damage, differentiate from RA | Shows **pencil-in-cup** erosion (central erosion with adjacent bone proliferation), **periostitis**, **bony ankylosis**, **asymmetric sacroiliitis** |
| **MRI (with STIR)** | Bone marrow edema, enthesitis | Suspected early disease or axial involvement | High sensitivity for enthesitis/sacroiliitis; shows BME at entheses |
| **Ultrasound (Power Doppler)** | Synovitis, enthesitis, tenosynovitis | Bedside assessment, monitor response | Hypoechoic synovitis + doppler flow = active; enthesitis = thickened, hypoechoic tendon insertion |
| **Skin biopsy** (rarely needed) | Histology: Munro microabscesses, parakeratosis | Confirm psoriasis if skin unclear | If skin is atypical, biopsy nail fold or plaque |

**Single finding that confirms:** Classic **pencil-in-cup** deformity on X-ray (central erosion of phalangeal head + adjacent bone formation = pathognomonic). Clinical: dactylitis in a patient with psoriasis is enough for high suspicion.

**Single finding that rules out:** A positive anti-CCP with high titer and bilateral symmetric small joint involvement makes RA far more likely. But note: PsA and RA can coexist (rare) — check for psoriasis as a distinct entity.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Aim:** Control inflammation, prevent joint damage, preserve function, treat skin.

**Immediate (first hour):**  
Patient in ED with acute monarthritis — must differentiate from septic arthritis. If red-hot, painful, cannot bear weight → aspirate joint. If Gram stain + → start septic protocol before PsA therapy. If clear aspirate (WBC <50,000, crystals none, culture negative) → treat flare.

- **NSAID (naproxen 500 mg PO BD or ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS)** for symptomatic relief — but short term ONLY (max 7–14 days) due to renal/GI risk. Do not use in patients with impaired renal function or GI bleed history.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**  
Once diagnosis confirmed (or at least high probability) and infection excluded:

- **Corticosteroids:** Only for severe acute flare — **prednisolone 20–30 mg PO OD for 5–7 days**, then taper. Avoid long-term steroids (they worsen psoriasis and increase metabolic effects). Local steroid injection (triamcinolone 40 mg intra-articular) for mono/oligoarthritis.
- **Disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) initiation** — begin early to prevent progression. First-line conventional synthetic DMARD (csDMARD):
  - **Methotrexate 15–25 mg PO once weekly** (start at 10 mg, increase by 5 mg every 4 weeks, with folic acid 5 mg weekly day after MTX). Best for skin and joints. Monitor LFTs, FBC, Cr at baseline and q4-8 weeks.
  - Alternative: **Leflunomide 20 mg PO OD** if MTX intolerant or contraindicated.
- **Screen for TB** (Quantiferon/chest X-ray) before any biologic therapy.

**Long-term:**

- **Biologic DMARDs** (bDMARD) if inadequate response to ≥2 csDMARDs (e.g., MTX + leflunomide) after 3–6 months:
  - **First-line:** TNF inhibitors — **Adalimumab 40 mg SC every 2 weeks**, **Etanercept 50 mg SC weekly**, or **Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then q8 weeks**. These improve both skin and joints within 4–12 weeks.
  - If TNFi fails, switch to IL-17 inhibitor (Secukinumab 150–300 mg SC weekly x4 then monthly) or IL-12/23 inhibitor (Ustekinumab 45–90 mg SC at 0, 4 weeks then q12 weeks).
  - **Apremilast** (PDE4 inhibitor) 30 mg PO BD — milder disease, no biologic screen needed.
- **Role of corticosteroids:** avoid long-term; but intra-articular injections (e.g., methylprednisolone 40–80 mg in knee) are useful for oligoarticular flares.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do NOT start steroids without checking joint aspiration or considering infection first (especially if monarticular).
  - Do NOT use **hydroxychloroquine** — can flare psoriasis.
  - Do NOT use **sulfasalazine** as first-line (less effective for axial/enthesitis; occasional use for peripheral disease — 2–3 g/day).
  - Do NOT stop DMARDs abruptly in well-controlled patients — wean under specialist guidance (risk of rebound).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Psoriatic Arthritis | Rheumatoid Arthritis | Osteoarthritis | Gout |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|---------------|------|
| **Pain** | Inflammatory (morning stiffness >30 min) | Inflammatory | Mechanical (worse with use, better rest) | Acute, excruciating, red-hot |
| **Joint distribution** | Asymmetric oligoarthritis; dactylitis; spine/entheses | Symmetric polyarthritis (hands, wrists, MCPs, PIPs, not DIPs) | Weight-bearing joints (knees, hips, hands – DIPs, CMC thumb) | 1st MTP (podagra), ankle, knee |
| **Skin/nail** | Psoriasis (scalp, extensor surfaces), nail pitting/onycholysis | Rheumatoid nodules (~30%) | Heberden’s/Bouchard’s nodes | Tophi (if chronic) |
| **Labs** | RF-, anti-CCP- ; CRP/ESR may be normal | RF+, anti-CCP+ (85–95%) | Normal | Urate >6.0 mg/dL (but can be normal acutely) |
| **X-ray** | Pencil-in-cup, periostitis, asymmetric SI joint; new bone | Juxta-articular erosions, no new bone; symmetric | Osteophytes, joint space narrowing, no erosions | Punched-out erosions, overhanging edges |
| **Key differentiator** | Dactylitis + enthesitis + psoriasis + negative RF | Positive anti-CCP and symmetric MCP/wrist | No systemic inflammation | Polarized light microscopy: negative birefringent urate crystals |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Joint destruction (erosive disease, arthritis mutilans):**  
- Presents: progressive deformity, telescoping digits (shortening of phalanges due to bone resorption).  
- Key sign: X-ray shows severe pencil-in-cup, bone lysis, and ankylosis.  
- What to do: aggressive biologic therapy (TNFi or IL-17i) ± surgery (arthrodesis).  

**Axial involvement (sacroiliitis, syndesmophyte formation):**  
- Presents: chronic inflammatory back pain, reduced spinal mobility, possible progression to bamboo spine (as in AS).  
- Key sign: Schober test ≤4 cm, chest expansion <2.5 cm, positive HLA-B27.  
- What to do: PT, NSAIDs, TNFi.  

**Cardiovascular disease:**  
- Chronic systemic inflammation increases risk of MI, stroke, hypertension.  
- Screen: BP, lipid panel, diabetes screening annually. Manage aggressively (statin, antihypertensives).  

**Osteoporosis:**  
- Due to inflammation + corticosteroid use (if any).  
- Check DEXA scan after 3 months of systemic steroids or in postmenopausal women with active disease.  

**Depression/quality of life:**  
- Chronic pain, disfigurement, fatigue.  
- Screen with PHQ-9; refer to psychology/psychiatry if needed.  

**Late presentation (untreated):**  
Patient presents with advanced deformities, fused joints, and severe disability. They may have been treated for psoriasis only with topical agents. Key: ask about morning stiffness and joint swelling at every dermatology visit.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **PsA CLUE** (Diagnostic features to look for on exam):
  - **P** — Psoriasis (skin/nail)
  - **S** — Sausage digit (dactylitis)
  - **A** — Asymmetric oligoarthritis
  - **E** — Enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia)
- **PENCIL** (Pattern of bone damage on X-ray):
  - **P** — Periostitis
  - **E** — Erosion (central -> pencil-in-cup)
  - **N** — New bone formation (syndesmophytes)
  - **C** — Chondrocalcinosis? (not specific)
  - **I** — Iliosacral joint (asymmetric)
  - **L** — Loose body? (enthesophytes)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition):**

1. **Ask every psoriasis patient: "Do your hands or back feel stiff in the morning?"** — morning stiffness >30 min = red flag for PsA.
2. **Check nails** — even if patient denies psoriasis, look for nail pitting and onycholysis. Nail psoriasis is a strong predictor of PsA (up to 80% of PsA patients have nail involvement vs 10% of psoriasis without arthritis).
3. **Dactylitis is pathognomonic** — if you see a sausage digit and the patient has no infection (fever, raised WBC), think PsA first.
4. **Enthesitis is often missed** — tenderness at the Achilles insertion or plantar fascia origin is common in PsA; test by palpating the posterior calcaneus and the medial calcaneal tubercle.
5. **Don't trust normal CRP** — up to 40% of PsA patients have normal inflammatory markers even with active synovitis. Use ultrasound/MRI if suspicion high.
6. **X-ray hands/feet should include oblique views to see pencil-in-cup** — AP only may miss early erosions.
7. **Symmetric polyarthritis in a patient with psoriasis could be RA** — check anti-CCP; they can coexist. However, typical PsA asymmetry is a useful clue.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Not ddx dactylitis from infection or gout — aspirate if hot/red/tender and patient unwell.
- Starting steroids without aspiration for acute monarthritis (misses septic arthritis).
- Using hydroxychloroquine for mild PsA (can flare psoriasis).
- Not screening for TB before biologics (risk of reactivation — do Quantiferon and CXR).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (rapid review or rheumatology consult):**
- **NEWS2 ≥5** in a PsA patient on immunosuppression — could be sepsis (especially if on biologic or MTX).
- **Suspected septic arthritis** in a patient on DMARD/biologic — joint aspiration + immediate antibiotics + stop immunosuppressants.
- **Acute flare with inability to walk** — may need high-dose steroids or IV methylprednisolone (500–1000 mg IV x3 days) after infection excluded.
- **New onset visual symptoms (red eye, pain, photophobia)** — suspect iritis/uveitis (complication of spondyloarthropathy; treat with topical steroids; refer to ophthalmology).

**When to call ICU/crash team:**
- **Anaphylaxis** to infliximab or other biologic infusion.
- **Severe infection** (sepsis, septic shock) in an immunosuppressed patient — consider stopping biologic, start broad-spectrum antibiotics, ICU admission if lactate >2 mmol/L, vasopressor requirement.
- **Low GCS** (<12) in a patient with suspected meningitis — common in immunosuppression.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** or single parameter: RR>30, O2 sat <88%, SBP <90 mmHg, HR>130.

**Specific thresholds:**
- Lactate >2 mmol/L → escalate to senior; >4 mmol/L → ICU consult.
- GCS ≤8 → protect airway, call ICU.
- Neutrophil count <0.5 x10^9/L (on MTX) → stop MTX, sepsis bundle.

**When to call respiratory team:**
- New dyspnea, cough, fever in patient on anti-TNF → possible TB reactivation (consider bronchoscopy for TB culture) or invasive fungal infection.

**Summary for FY1:** If a PsA patient on DMARDs or biologics looks unwell (fever, tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status) — use NEWS2, take blood cultures, start broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam), stop immunosuppressants after discussion with rheumatology, and escalate aggressively. Don't wait for test results.

## Psoriatic Arthritis

**One-liner:** Inflammatory arthritis in patients with psoriasis — often oligoarticular, with dactylitis and enthesitis; can erode joints and cause disability if missed.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 35-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of pain and swelling in her right index finger and left knee. She has a scaly, silvery rash on her elbows and scalp, which she’s had for years. On exam, the finger is diffusely swollen (“sausage digit”) and tender along the tendon sheath. There is tenderness over the insertion of the left Achilles tendon. She reports morning stiffness >30 minutes but feels better after moving. CRP is mildly elevated (15 mg/L), RF and anti-CCP are negative.

**Dissect the vignette:**
- **Scaly, silvery rash on elbows/scalp** → psoriasis (often precedes arthritis by years)
- **Swelling in one finger, entire digit** → dactylitis (“sausage digit”) — hallmark of PsA, distinct from RA
- **Tenderness at Achilles insertion** → enthesitis (another PsA hallmark, uncommon in RA or OA)
- **Morning stiffness >30 min** → inflammatory arthritis (versus mechanical pain of OA)
- **Asymmetric oligoarthritis** (right index + left knee) → typical PsA pattern (vs symmetric small joint RA)
- **RF/anti-CCP negative** → seronegative spondyloarthropathy; helps rule out RA
- **CRP mildly raised** → active inflammation, but can be normal in PsA

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Axial-only PsA** (~5%): presents as inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness, improves with exercise, no improvement with rest) with no peripheral joint involvement. Can be mistaken for ankylosing spondylitis — look for psoriasis (often hidden in scalp, gluteal cleft, or nails) and check HLA-B27 (positive in ~40% of PsA with axial disease).
- **PsA sine psoriasis** (~15%): arthritis typical of PsA (dactylitis, enthesitis, nail pitting) but no skin lesions at presentation. Family history of psoriasis or nail changes (pitting, onycholysis) are key clues.
- **Juvenile-onset PsA**: presents with oligoarthritis and dactylitis in a child, often before skin psoriasis appears. Can be misdiagnosed as JIA — nail pitting and family history essential.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Psoriatic arthritis is a chronic inflammatory arthritis that affects people with psoriasis (a common skin condition). It causes joint pain, stiffness, and swelling, and if untreated can lead to permanent joint damage and disability. It also affects the entheses (where tendons attach to bone) and can cause spondylitis.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** Affects up to 30% of people with psoriasis (global prevalence ~0.3–1% of population). Many patients with psoriasis do not know they have arthritis — you must actively screen.
- **Stakes:** Untreated, it leads to erosive, deforming arthritis (arthritis mutilans), significant functional loss, reduced quality of life, and increased cardiovascular risk (systemic inflammation).
- **Common pitfalls:**
  - Mistaking dactylitis for gout or infection.
  - Assuming arthritis in a patient with psoriasis is osteoarthritis (age-related) or rheumatoid arthritis — get X-rays and serology correct.
  - Missing axial disease (inflammatory back pain) because patient doesn’t mention back — ask.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:**  
Psoriatic arthritis sits within the spondyloarthropathy family — diseases driven by **interleukin-17 (IL-17)** and **IL-23** produced by Th17 cells. In a genetically susceptible person (HLA-Cw6, HLA-B27, other loci), environmental triggers (trauma, infection, stress) activate dendritic cells that present self-peptides (e.g., from cartilage) to T cells. This creates an IL-23/IL-17 axis: IL-23 drives Th17 cell expansion, which then secretes IL-17. IL-17 recruits neutrophils and stimulates fibroblasts and osteoclasts, causing **synovitis, enthesitis, and new bone formation**.

**Mechanism → sign:**
- **IL-17 driven inflammation** → synovitis → swollen, warm joint.
- **Enthesitis** (IL-17 stimulates inflammation at the osteotendinous junction) → pain at Achilles, plantar fascia, etc.
- **Dactylitis**: inflammation throughout a whole digit — synovitis, tenosynovitis, and enthesitis simultaneously — causing the "sausage" swelling.
- **New bone formation** (periostitis, syndesmophytes) — paradoxically, PsA causes both **erosion** (by osteoclast activation via RANKL) and **proliferation** (by IL-17-driven osteoblast activity). This is unique: in RA you see only erosion; in PsA you see erosion AND bony outgrowths, especially in the hands/feet (pencil-in-cup deformities) and spine (asymmetric paravertebral ossification).
- **Nail disease** (pitting, onycholysis, hyperkeratosis): the nail is a modified skin appendage; IL-17-driven inflammation in the nail matrix causes the same psoriatic pathology — hence nail psoriasis correlates strongly with enthesitis/arthritis.

**Connect to pattern:**
- Asymmetric oligoarthritis → because PsA tends to involve a limited number of "target" joints, often in a streaming pattern (e.g., one hand and the opposite knee). Reason unknown but thought to be related to triggered memory T cells homing to sites of previous microtrauma.
- Erythematous scaly plaques → skin inflammation also driven by IL-23/Th17 axis, but arthritis can occur without severe skin disease (cytokine trafficking differences).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Examples | Stem clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common) | No clear trigger; genetic predisposition + random event | Patient with long-standing psoriasis develops joint pain for no apparent reason. |
| **Infectious** | Streptococcal pharyngitis, HIV, trauma? (post-traumatic PsA) | "Had a sore throat 2 weeks ago" — gut microbiome also implicated. |
| **Trauma** | "Deep Koebner" phenomenon — physical injury precipitates arthritis in a predisposed joint | "Pain started after I twisted my ankle" — but systemic signs later. |
| **Drugs** (rare) | TNF inhibitors used for other diseases (paradoxical PsA) | Anti-TNF therapy for Crohn's → new onset PsA. |
| **Endocrine** (rare) | Post-pregnancy, stress | "Arthritis started while under huge work stress." |

**Most common cause:** Idiopathic, in a patient with existing psoriasis. About 75% have skin disease before arthritis. Screening every psoriatic patient for joint symptoms = key.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** Use the CASPAR criteria for PsA classification (most specific):
- Patient must have inflammatory articular disease (joint, spine, or entheseal) **AND** ≥3 of:
  - Current psoriasis (2 points) OR personal history of psoriasis (1) OR family history (1)
  - Psoriatic nail dystrophy (pitting, onycholysis, hyperkeratosis) — 1 point
  - Negative RF — 1 point
  - Dactylitis (current or history) — 1 point
  - Radiographic evidence of juxta-articular new bone formation (on X-ray of hand/foot) — 1 point
- Score ≥3 points = classified as PsA.

**Test-by-test pattern:**
| Test | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|---------------|
| **RF, anti-CCP** | Autoantibodies | Rule out RA | Negative in >90% PsA (but can be false positive in older patients — use clinical judgment) |
| **CRP / ESR** | Inflammatory markers | Assess activity, monitor treatment | Raised in active disease; can be normal in ~40% — don't rely on normal CRP to exclude PsA |
| **HLA-B27** | Genetic marker | Suspect axial disease; prognostic | Positive in 40% with axial PsA (vs 90% in AS); has limited diagnostic value alone |
| **X-ray hands/feet** | Erosions, new bone formation | Detect joint damage, differentiate from RA | Shows **pencil-in-cup** erosion (central erosion with adjacent bone proliferation), **periostitis**, **bony ankylosis**, **asymmetric sacroiliitis** |
| **MRI (with STIR)** | Bone marrow edema, enthesitis | Suspected early disease or axial involvement | High sensitivity for enthesitis/sacroiliitis; shows BME at entheses |
| **Ultrasound (Power Doppler)** | Synovitis, enthesitis, tenosynovitis | Bedside assessment, monitor response | Hypoechoic synovitis + doppler flow = active; enthesitis = thickened, hypoechoic tendon insertion |
| **Skin biopsy** (rarely needed) | Histology: Munro microabscesses, parakeratosis | Confirm psoriasis if skin unclear | If skin is atypical, biopsy nail fold or plaque |

**Single finding that confirms:** Classic **pencil-in-cup** deformity on X-ray (central erosion of phalangeal head + adjacent bone formation = pathognomonic). Clinical: dactylitis in a patient with psoriasis is enough for high suspicion.

**Single finding that rules out:** A positive anti-CCP with high titer and bilateral symmetric small joint involvement makes RA far more likely. But note: PsA and RA can coexist (rare) — check for psoriasis as a distinct entity.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Aim:** Control inflammation, prevent joint damage, preserve function, treat skin.

**Immediate (first hour):**  
Patient in ED with acute monarthritis — must differentiate from septic arthritis. If red-hot, painful, cannot bear weight → aspirate joint. If Gram stain + → start septic protocol before PsA therapy. If clear aspirate (WBC <50,000, crystals none, culture negative) → treat flare.

- **NSAID (naproxen 500 mg PO BD or ibuprofen 400 mg PO TDS)** for symptomatic relief — but short term ONLY (max 7–14 days) due to renal/GI risk. Do not use in patients with impaired renal function or GI bleed history.

**Ward (24–72 hours):**  
Once diagnosis confirmed (or at least high probability) and infection excluded:

- **Corticosteroids:** Only for severe acute flare — **prednisolone 20–30 mg PO OD for 5–7 days**, then taper. Avoid long-term steroids (they worsen psoriasis and increase metabolic effects). Local steroid injection (triamcinolone 40 mg intra-articular) for mono/oligoarthritis.
- **Disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) initiation** — begin early to prevent progression. First-line conventional synthetic DMARD (csDMARD):
  - **Methotrexate 15–25 mg PO once weekly** (start at 10 mg, increase by 5 mg every 4 weeks, with folic acid 5 mg weekly day after MTX). Best for skin and joints. Monitor LFTs, FBC, Cr at baseline and q4-8 weeks.
  - Alternative: **Leflunomide 20 mg PO OD** if MTX intolerant or contraindicated.
- **Screen for TB** (Quantiferon/chest X-ray) before any biologic therapy.

**Long-term:**

- **Biologic DMARDs** (bDMARD) if inadequate response to ≥2 csDMARDs (e.g., MTX + leflunomide) after 3–6 months:
  - **First-line:** TNF inhibitors — **Adalimumab 40 mg SC every 2 weeks**, **Etanercept 50 mg SC weekly**, or **Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks then q8 weeks**. These improve both skin and joints within 4–12 weeks.
  - If TNFi fails, switch to IL-17 inhibitor (Secukinumab 150–300 mg SC weekly x4 then monthly) or IL-12/23 inhibitor (Ustekinumab 45–90 mg SC at 0, 4 weeks then q12 weeks).
  - **Apremilast** (PDE4 inhibitor) 30 mg PO BD — milder disease, no biologic screen needed.
- **Role of corticosteroids:** avoid long-term; but intra-articular injections (e.g., methylprednisolone 40–80 mg in knee) are useful for oligoarticular flares.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do NOT start steroids without checking joint aspiration or considering infection first (especially if monarticular).
  - Do NOT use **hydroxychloroquine** — can flare psoriasis.
  - Do NOT use **sulfasalazine** as first-line (less effective for axial/enthesitis; occasional use for peripheral disease — 2–3 g/day).
  - Do NOT stop DMARDs abruptly in well-controlled patients — wean under specialist guidance (risk of rebound).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Psoriatic Arthritis | Rheumatoid Arthritis | Osteoarthritis | Gout |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|---------------|------|
| **Pain** | Inflammatory (morning stiffness >30 min) | Inflammatory | Mechanical (worse with use, better rest) | Acute, excruciating, red-hot |
| **Joint distribution** | Asymmetric oligoarthritis; dactylitis; spine/entheses | Symmetric polyarthritis (hands, wrists, MCPs, PIPs, not DIPs) | Weight-bearing joints (knees, hips, hands – DIPs, CMC thumb) | 1st MTP (podagra), ankle, knee |
| **Skin/nail** | Psoriasis (scalp, extensor surfaces), nail pitting/onycholysis | Rheumatoid nodules (~30%) | Heberden’s/Bouchard’s nodes | Tophi (if chronic) |
| **Labs** | RF-, anti-CCP- ; CRP/ESR may be normal | RF+, anti-CCP+ (85–95%) | Normal | Urate >6.0 mg/dL (but can be normal acutely) |
| **X-ray** | Pencil-in-cup, periostitis, asymmetric SI joint; new bone | Juxta-articular erosions, no new bone; symmetric | Osteophytes, joint space narrowing, no erosions | Punched-out erosions, overhanging edges |
| **Key differentiator** | Dactylitis + enthesitis + psoriasis + negative RF | Positive anti-CCP and symmetric MCP/wrist | No systemic inflammation | Polarized light microscopy: negative birefringent urate crystals |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Joint destruction (erosive disease, arthritis mutilans):**  
- Presents: progressive deformity, telescoping digits (shortening of phalanges due to bone resorption).  
- Key sign: X-ray shows severe pencil-in-cup, bone lysis, and ankylosis.  
- What to do: aggressive biologic therapy (TNFi or IL-17i) ± surgery (arthrodesis).  

**Axial involvement (sacroiliitis, syndesmophyte formation):**  
- Presents: chronic inflammatory back pain, reduced spinal mobility, possible progression to bamboo spine (as in AS).  
- Key sign: Schober test ≤4 cm, chest expansion <2.5 cm, positive HLA-B27.  
- What to do: PT, NSAIDs, TNFi.  

**Cardiovascular disease:**  
- Chronic systemic inflammation increases risk of MI, stroke, hypertension.  
- Screen: BP, lipid panel, diabetes screening annually. Manage aggressively (statin, antihypertensives).  

**Osteoporosis:**  
- Due to inflammation + corticosteroid use (if any).  
- Check DEXA scan after 3 months of systemic steroids or in postmenopausal women with active disease.  

**Depression/quality of life:**  
- Chronic pain, disfigurement, fatigue.  
- Screen with PHQ-9; refer to psychology/psychiatry if needed.  

**Late presentation (untreated):**  
Patient presents with advanced deformities, fused joints, and severe disability. They may have been treated for psoriasis only with topical agents. Key: ask about morning stiffness and joint swelling at every dermatology visit.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **PsA CLUE** (Diagnostic features to look for on exam):
  - **P** — Psoriasis (skin/nail)
  - **S** — Sausage digit (dactylitis)
  - **A** — Asymmetric oligoarthritis
  - **E** — Enthesitis (Achilles, plantar fascia)
- **PENCIL** (Pattern of bone damage on X-ray):
  - **P** — Periostitis
  - **E** — Erosion (central -> pencil-in-cup)
  - **N** — New bone formation (syndesmophytes)
  - **C** — Chondrocalcinosis? (not specific)
  - **I** — Iliosacral joint (asymmetric)
  - **L** — Loose body? (enthesophytes)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition):**

1. **Ask every psoriasis patient: "Do your hands or back feel stiff in the morning?"** — morning stiffness >30 min = red flag for PsA.
2. **Check nails** — even if patient denies psoriasis, look for nail pitting and onycholysis. Nail psoriasis is a strong predictor of PsA (up to 80% of PsA patients have nail involvement vs 10% of psoriasis without arthritis).
3. **Dactylitis is pathognomonic** — if you see a sausage digit and the patient has no infection (fever, raised WBC), think PsA first.
4. **Enthesitis is often missed** — tenderness at the Achilles insertion or plantar fascia origin is common in PsA; test by palpating the posterior calcaneus and the medial calcaneal tubercle.
5. **Don't trust normal CRP** — up to 40% of PsA patients have normal inflammatory markers even with active synovitis. Use ultrasound/MRI if suspicion high.
6. **X-ray hands/feet should include oblique views to see pencil-in-cup** — AP only may miss early erosions.
7. **Symmetric polyarthritis in a patient with psoriasis could be RA** — check anti-CCP; they can coexist. However, typical PsA asymmetry is a useful clue.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Not ddx dactylitis from infection or gout — aspirate if hot/red/tender and patient unwell.
- Starting steroids without aspiration for acute monarthritis (misses septic arthritis).
- Using hydroxychloroquine for mild PsA (can flare psoriasis).
- Not screening for TB before biologics (risk of reactivation — do Quantiferon and CXR).

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (rapid review or rheumatology consult):**
- **NEWS2 ≥5** in a PsA patient on immunosuppression — could be sepsis (especially if on biologic or MTX).
- **Suspected septic arthritis** in a patient on DMARD/biologic — joint aspiration + immediate antibiotics + stop immunosuppressants.
- **Acute flare with inability to walk** — may need high-dose steroids or IV methylprednisolone (500–1000 mg IV x3 days) after infection excluded.
- **New onset visual symptoms (red eye, pain, photophobia)** — suspect iritis/uveitis (complication of spondyloarthropathy; treat with topical steroids; refer to ophthalmology).

**When to call ICU/crash team:**
- **Anaphylaxis** to infliximab or other biologic infusion.
- **Severe infection** (sepsis, septic shock) in an immunosuppressed patient — consider stopping biologic, start broad-spectrum antibiotics, ICU admission if lactate >2 mmol/L, vasopressor requirement.
- **Low GCS** (<12) in a patient with suspected meningitis — common in immunosuppression.
- **NEWS2 ≥7** or single parameter: RR>30, O2 sat <88%, SBP <90 mmHg, HR>130.

**Specific thresholds:**
- Lactate >2 mmol/L → escalate to senior; >4 mmol/L → ICU consult.
- GCS ≤8 → protect airway, call ICU.
- Neutrophil count <0.5 x10^9/L (on MTX) → stop MTX, sepsis bundle.

**When to call respiratory team:**
- New dyspnea, cough, fever in patient on anti-TNF → possible TB reactivation (consider bronchoscopy for TB culture) or invasive fungal infection.

**Summary for FY1:** If a PsA patient on DMARDs or biologics looks unwell (fever, tachycardia, hypotension, altered mental status) — use NEWS2, take blood cultures, start broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam), stop immunosuppressants after discussion with rheumatology, and escalate aggressively. Don't wait for test results.
